does: This paper argues for a shift from artifact-centric to systems-level assessment of value alignment in AI-equipped sociotechnical systems. Rather than focusing narrowly on how individual algorithmic decision-making tools violate norms around fairness, privacy, or safety, the work advocates for understanding misalignment as an emergent property of entire systems encompassing technology, institutions, and stakeholders. Using fairness research as a primary concrete domain, the paper demonstrates how value misalignments often arise not from isolated algorithmic failures but from structural interactions between multiple system components, governance gaps, and competing stakeholder interests that traditional artifact-focused auditing misses.
connects: This work builds on and responds to the rapidly growing AI ethics and algorithmic fairness literature that has documented numerous failures in deployed systems. It extends critiques of technical accountability frameworks by drawing on sociotechnical systems theory and systems thinking traditions, which emphasize how outcomes emerge from interactions among diverse elements rather than from isolated components. The paper engages with decades of research in fairness, transparency, and safety in AI while arguing these literatures have been too narrowly focused on individual algorithm properties. It positions itself against purely technical or algorithm-centric approaches, instead integrating insights from organizational behavior, governance studies, and values in design.
enables: This systems perspective opens pathways for developing more robust assessment methodologies that can diagnose value misalignment across institutional and organizational boundaries. It enables future work on designing governance structures, audit protocols, and accountability mechanisms that address systemic rather than purely technical failures. The framework points toward better tools for tracing how values get lost or distorted through organizational processes, human decision-making layers, and technical infrastructure interactions. It suggests that effective value alignment requires coordinated intervention across system levels—combining technical improvements with institutional redesign, stakeholder engagement practices, and governance reforms rather than treating these as separate domains.
